content.text: Uploading a file to Claude.ai extends a conversation beyond plain text — instead of manually copying document contents into the message box, you attach the file and Claude reads it directly, working from the full original content without the loss or formatting errors that copy-paste often introduces.
What File Uploads Are

File uploads in Claude.ai are a feature that lets you attach documents, images, data files, and code files to a conversation message. Claude processes the uploaded content as additional context alongside your text prompt. The uploaded file becomes part of the conversation turn, and Claude can read, analyze, summarize, or answer questions about it.

The feature is available in Claude.ai (the web and mobile interfaces) for all plan tiers, though limits on the number of files, file size, and file types differ between Free, Pro, and Team/Enterprise plans. Files are uploaded directly from your device and temporarily stored for the duration of the session.

attrs.alt: Overview of file uploads in Claude.ai

content.text: Why File Uploads Matter

Most knowledge work involves documents — PDFs, spreadsheets, research papers, codebases, and data exports. Without file upload capability, using Claude with these artifacts means either pasting their contents (lossy and tedious for large files) or describing them (imprecise and slow). File uploads eliminate both problems.

For analytical tasks, uploading a CSV or spreadsheet lets Claude read the actual data rather than a summary you have described. For code review, uploading a file lets Claude see the exact syntax rather than a transcription with potential errors. For research, uploading a PDF lets Claude work from the original paper rather than your paraphrase of it.
Supported File Formats
Documents
PDF (.pdf)
— the most commonly used document format; Claude reads text content and can process tables and structured content in most PDFs
Microsoft Word (.docx, .doc)
— Word documents including formatted text, tables, and embedded lists
Plain text (.txt)
— raw text files without formatting
Rich Text Format (.rtf)
— formatted text documents
Markdown (.md)
— Markdown-formatted text files
Spreadsheets and Data
CSV (.csv)
— comma-separated values, the most Claude-friendly format for tabular data
Microsoft Excel (.xlsx, .xls)
— spreadsheets with potentially multiple sheets
JSON (.json)
— structured data in JSON format
XML (.xml)
— markup-based data files
Code Files

Claude accepts most common programming language source files — Python (.py), JavaScript (.js), TypeScript (.ts), Java (.java), C/C++ (.c, .cpp, .h), Go (.go), Rust (.rs), Ruby (.rb), PHP (.php), Swift (.swift), Kotlin (.kt), SQL (.sql), shell scripts (.sh, .bash), and configuration files (.yaml, .yml, .toml, .env examples). The exact list may expand over time.
Images
JPEG / JPG
— photographs and lossy-compressed images
PNG
— screenshots, diagrams, and images requiring transparency
GIF
— static or animated images (Claude reads static frames)
WEBP
— modern web image format
SVG
— vector graphics files (treated as text/XML that Claude can also read structurally)

attrs.alt: Step-by-step flow from file selection to conversation context

content.text: How to Upload Files
Using the Paperclip / Attach Button
The most reliable upload method is the attachment button in Claude.ai's message input area. It appears as a paperclip icon or a plus (+) button depending on your interface version and device.
Click the paperclip or attachment icon at the bottom-left of the message input box.
A file picker dialog opens. Navigate to the file you want to upload.
Select one or more files (hold Ctrl/Cmd to select multiple).
The files appear as attachment chips in the message input area.
Type your prompt (or leave it blank if you want Claude to describe what it sees).
Press Enter or click Send.
Drag and Drop

Claude.ai supports dragging files from your file system directly onto the message input area. Drag any supported file from Finder, Windows Explorer, or your file manager and drop it on the conversation window. The file attaches immediately and you can then type your prompt before sending.
Copy-Paste for Images

For images, you can paste directly from the clipboard using Ctrl+V / Cmd+V in the message input. This is particularly useful for screenshots — take a screenshot with your OS's screenshot tool, switch to Claude.ai, and paste. The image appears as an attachment without needing to save it to disk first.
Mobile Upload

On the Claude.ai mobile app, the attachment button opens the system's native file picker, which gives access to Files (iOS) or the Downloads folder (Android), your photo library, and cloud storage services like iCloud Drive or Google Drive, depending on your device's integrations.
File Size and Count Limits
Anthropic imposes limits on file uploads to manage server load and API context constraints. Limits are approximate and may be updated:
Plan
Max File Size
Files per Message
Daily Upload Limit
Free
~10 MB
Limited
Pro
~30 MB per file
Higher
Team / Enterprise
Varies by config
Higher
By agreement

The more significant constraint for large files is Claude's context window. Even if a PDF uploads successfully, Claude can only work with what fits in its context window. For very long documents (hundreds of pages), Claude may process a subset of the content or summarize sections that don't fit. Splitting large documents into chunks often yields better results for very long source materials.
How Claude Processes Uploaded Files
Text Extraction

When you upload a PDF or Word document, Claude.ai's backend extracts the text content before sending it to Claude as part of the prompt. Claude receives the text, not the binary file itself. This means that scanned PDFs — images of text without OCR — may not be readable unless the scanning application embedded text recognition. Searchable PDFs (created from digital sources or with OCR applied) work well.
Image Analysis

When you upload an image, Claude uses its vision capability to analyze the image content. Claude can read text in images (OCR-like capability), identify objects and scenes, describe diagram structure, and extract data from tables or charts shown in screenshots. The model processes the image as pixels, so the quality and resolution of the image affects what Claude can read accurately.
Code and Data Files

Source code and data files are passed to Claude as plain text with their file extension noted. Claude uses the file extension and content to infer the language or data format and provides syntax-aware analysis. For CSV and JSON data, Claude reads the structure and values and can perform calculations, summarize datasets, or write code to process them further.
What You Can Do With Uploaded Files
Document Analysis
Summarize a long PDF report into bullet points covering the main findings
Extract specific data points from a table in a document
Compare two documents and highlight differences or contradictions
Identify action items or commitments in meeting transcripts
Answer specific questions about a document's content
Data Work
Describe the structure of a CSV file and its column contents
Find patterns, outliers, or anomalies in a dataset
Write Python or SQL code to process the uploaded data further
Calculate summary statistics from numerical data columns
Transform data from one format to another (CSV to JSON, for example)
Code Review and Assistance
Review a code file for bugs, security issues, or style problems
Explain what a complex function or module does
Suggest refactoring improvements with before/after examples
Add docstrings or comments to undocumented code
Generate unit tests for uploaded functions
Image Tasks
Read and transcribe text from a screenshot or photo
Describe a diagram or flowchart and explain its structure
Extract data from a chart or graph shown in an image
Identify objects, scenes, or elements in a photograph
Analyze UI screenshots for design feedback
Working With Multiple Files

You can attach multiple files in a single message. Claude reads all attached files and can compare, cross-reference, or synthesize information across them. For example, you can upload two versions of a document and ask Claude to identify what changed, or upload a specification document alongside a code file and ask whether the implementation matches the spec.

# Example multi-file prompts

# Comparing documents
[Attach: Q3_report.pdf, Q4_report.pdf]
"Compare the revenue figures between Q3 and Q4.
 What changed and what were the major drivers?"

# Code meets spec
[Attach: api-spec.yaml, server.py]
"Does the Python implementation match the API specification?
 List any endpoints that are missing or don't match the spec."

# Data analysis
[Attach: customers.csv, orders.csv]
"How many customers placed more than 3 orders?
 Write a pandas snippet that computes this from these two files."
Tips for Getting the Best Results
Prepare Files Before Uploading

Searchable, well-structured files give better results. If you have a scanned PDF, run it through an OCR tool (such as Adobe Acrobat, PDF24, or Tesseract) before uploading. For Excel files with multiple sheets, consider exporting only the relevant sheet as a CSV to reduce complexity.
Write Specific Prompts

Uploading a file without a prompt will prompt Claude to describe or summarize it, which is useful but general. For better results, write a specific question or instruction alongside the file: 'Find all mentions of the word deadline in this document' or 'What are the three most common error codes in this log file?'
Reference File Contents in Follow-Up Messages

Once a file is uploaded, Claude retains it for the duration of the conversation (within the context window). You can ask follow-up questions that refer back to the document without re-uploading it: 'Now summarize section 3 of the report I uploaded earlier.'
For Long Documents, Provide Page Guidance

If you know the relevant section is on pages 10–15 of a 200-page PDF, say so in the prompt. Claude will still process the full document but your guidance helps it focus. 'The methodology section is on pages 12–18. Summarize it in four bullet points.'
Common Mistakes
Uploading Scanned PDFs Expecting Text Extraction

A PDF created by scanning physical pages is an image inside a PDF container, not a text document. Claude cannot reliably read it without embedded OCR text. Check whether your PDF is searchable by pressing Ctrl+A / Cmd+A inside your PDF viewer — if you can select and copy text, the PDF is searchable; if nothing highlights, it likely needs OCR processing first.
Expecting Claude to Remember Files Across Sessions

Files uploaded in one conversation are not available in a new conversation. Each conversation starts fresh. If you need Claude to work with the same files repeatedly, either re-upload them each session or save your prepared prompts that include file context in a Project with project knowledge files (which persist across sessions).
Uploading Very Large Files Expecting Complete Analysis

Claude has a context window limit. A 500-page PDF uploaded as text may exceed what Claude can hold in a single context. For very long documents, split the analysis across multiple messages or conversations, or extract only the relevant sections before uploading.
Security and Privacy

Files uploaded to Claude.ai are transmitted to Anthropic's servers and processed there. Anthropic's privacy policy governs how this data is handled. By default, Anthropic may use conversation data including uploaded files to improve its models unless you opt out or use a plan tier (Team, Enterprise) that provides data privacy guarantees.

Do not upload files containing passwords, private keys, highly sensitive personal data, or confidential trade secrets unless you are on an Enterprise plan with appropriate data processing agreements. For sensitive code review, consider removing hardcoded credentials from the file before uploading.
Best Practices
Use CSV over Excel where possible
— CSV files are simpler, smaller, and more reliably parsed
Keep prompts paired with files
— always include a specific instruction alongside a file, not just the file alone
Use Projects for persistent files
— if you need the same document available across many sessions, add it to a Claude.ai Project's knowledge base
Split large documents
— for documents longer than 100 pages, extract and upload only the relevant sections
Verify image clarity
— for screenshots containing text, ensure sufficient resolution so characters are distinct
Use follow-up messages
— a conversation's file context persists, so use multiple messages to explore a large document progressively
Integrating File Uploads With the Projects Feature

Standalone file uploads apply to a single conversation and are not available in future sessions. For files that need to be available consistently — a reference document, a style guide, a configuration file — uploading them to a Claude.ai project's knowledge base is a better approach than attaching them to individual messages.

The distinction matters in practice: when you upload a file in a chat message, that file is visible only in that conversation and only while the conversation's context window has not been exceeded. When the same file is added to a project's knowledge base, it is loaded automatically into every new conversation in that project, from the very first message.
Message attachment
— visible in that conversation only, useful for one-off tasks
Project knowledge upload
— available in all project conversations, better for recurring reference material

To move from message-level to project-level file access, navigate to the project, open the Knowledge section, and upload the file there. Any file you frequently re-upload to conversations in the same domain is a good candidate for project knowledge.
File Upload in API Calls

When using Claude via the Anthropic API directly — rather than via Claude.ai — file uploads work through the messages API. Images can be sent as base64-encoded data in the content array alongside text. For document content, the typical approach is to extract the text client-side and include it as a text content block in the message.

For text-based documents (PDFs, Word files, CSVs), client-side text extraction using libraries like PyMuPDF for PDFs or pandas for CSV is the standard API pattern. The extracted text is passed as a text content block. This gives full programmatic control over what content Claude sees, which is useful for preprocessing steps like redacting sensitive fields before sending.
Batch Processing With File Uploads

When you need Claude to process many files — hundreds of documents in a classification or summarization pipeline — use Anthropic's batch API instead of individual API calls. The batch API allows you to submit large numbers of requests at once, processed asynchronously at a lower cost than synchronous calls. This is appropriate for non-real-time workflows like nightly ingestion of new reports or processing a backlog of customer feedback files.
Key Takeaways
File uploads in Claude.ai let you attach documents, images, spreadsheets, and code files directly to messages, giving Claude full access to the content without manual copy-paste.
Supported formats include PDF, DOCX, CSV, JSON, most image formats, and common source code files; exact limits depend on your plan tier.
Files can be uploaded via the attachment button, drag-and-drop, or clipboard paste for images.
Claude extracts text from documents, analyzes images using its vision capability, and reads code and data files as plain text with format awareness.
Scanned PDFs without embedded OCR text may not be readable — use searchable PDFs for best results.
Files persist in a conversation's context window but are not retained across separate sessions; use Project Knowledge for files that should be available in multiple sessions.
For sensitive content, review your plan's data privacy terms before uploading confidential files.
How to Use This Feature Effectively

Getting the most out of File Uploads requires understanding both its capabilities and its limitations. This section provides concrete guidance on using the feature to its full potential, with practical examples you can apply immediately.

The key to effective use of File Uploads is to start with clear intentions. Know what outcome you're aiming for before you begin, and set up the feature in a way that supports that outcome. Many users find it helpful to experiment with different approaches to discover what works best for their specific workflow.
Define your goal clearly before you start — vague intentions lead to vague results
Use the feature consistently to build familiarity and identify patterns
Combine with other Claude features for maximum effectiveness
Review outputs critically and provide feedback through follow-up prompts
Document what works for your use case so you can replicate success
Share knowledge with teammates to build organizational competency
Use Cases and Applications
There are many valuable use cases for File Uploads across different contexts. Understanding the range of applications helps you identify where this feature can add the most value to your workflow.
Professional productivity: Streamline repetitive tasks and focus on higher-value work
Research and analysis: Gather and synthesize information more efficiently
Content creation: Generate first drafts, outlines, and creative ideas
Learning and education: Explore new topics and get explanations of complex concepts
Problem-solving: Work through challenges with an AI thinking partner
Code assistance: Write, review, debug, and explain code
Communication: Draft, edit, and polish written communications
Integration with Your Workflow
Integrating File Uploads into your existing workflow is usually straightforward, but there are several strategies that can make the transition smoother and ensure you get maximum value.

Start by identifying the specific tasks where you'll use this feature most. Then create a small experiment to test how it fits into your existing process. Adjust your workflow based on what you learn, and gradually expand usage as you build confidence.
Map out your current workflow before adding Claude to identify integration points
Start with one specific task to build confidence before expanding usage
Create templates or prompts that you can reuse across similar tasks
Set up a feedback loop to continuously improve your approach
Track time savings and quality improvements to build the business case
Configuration and Settings

Configuring File Uploads correctly for your needs is important for getting consistent, high-quality results. While the defaults work well for most use cases, understanding the available options allows you to fine-tune the behavior.

Take time to explore the settings and options available. Each setting serves a specific purpose, and understanding what each one does gives you greater control over the output. Don't be afraid to experiment — you can always revert to defaults if needed.
Review all available settings before assuming defaults are optimal for your use case
Make changes incrementally so you can understand the impact of each adjustment
Save your preferred configurations for easy reuse across sessions
Test configuration changes with representative examples before relying on them in production
Document your configuration choices and the reasons behind them
Tips and Tricks

Experienced users of File Uploads have discovered many tips and tricks that significantly improve their results. These insights often come from experimentation and aren't always obvious from the documentation.
Use specific, concrete language rather than vague requests for better results
Break complex tasks into smaller steps for more reliable outcomes
Provide context and examples when working on specialized or domain-specific tasks
Use follow-up prompts to refine and improve initial outputs
Combine this feature with other Claude capabilities for compound benefits
Learn from both successes and failures to continuously improve your approach
Share tips with your team to build collective expertise
Summary

This guide covered File Uploads in depth, including how to use it effectively, practical applications across different contexts, integration strategies, configuration tips, and expert tricks. With this knowledge, you're well-equipped to make the most of this capability in your daily work.
